sentences.text: Yes.
Oh, we were there about a week.
No, no.
I went to school in the Rhineland near Bonn, Konigswinter.
Before-- I mean, it was the same summer.
My father left me, and then he came back, picked me up, and we went to Berlin.
Yes.
But I mean, usually it wasn't done.
The French didn't do it.
And beside, I was quite young.
But he knew a business associate that took me in.
And I went to school.
In Konigswinter.
No, it was just maybe, I don't know, six weeks, something like that.
In retrospect-- he never told me what he was doing.
And as he-- I never asked questions.
No, we were in a hotel.
In a hotel.
You also mentioned that this was the period of the Olympics and the Franco, the war in Spain.
What did you know about all of this?
I knew that I didn't like my life.
I mean, I liked my life with my parents, but I had a big anguish, mostly when Hitler spoke.
My father could listen and understand me.
I didn't understand everything.
But I could see how the people stopped, most of the people in Berlin.
And you could-- I sensed the fear and the hysterics.
Now when I look back, and I see him looking so ridiculous, like a clown, I felt, how could people be mesmerized by somebody so stupid?
But at the time, you didn't feel that way.
I had fear.
Did you and your father talk about it?
Not really.
My father-- my parents never spoke of their business.
We never asked questions.
Politics my--
World politics, very little.
Just my father said he was a dangerous man, and we might have a war again.
He said that.
But he said it was none of my business.
I should go back to my studies.
And I was a little girl.
And that-- OK.
Well, Franco, we-- of course, we were on the side of the Republicans.
And of course, we hated it.
But we never had much sympathy for Spain, you know, the Spain-- or the Italians.
The French don't like too much the southern neighbors.
The French like the French above everything else.
Oh, yes.
Yes, we did.
My parents-- but I was not allowed to turn it down.
When I see kids now turning on the television, opening the fridge, it's something we never did.
We ate together, but the radio was--
--listening after having my father's office in a mini studio.
And we were doing our homework.
We had homework until 10 o'clock every night.
So we knew not too much about it.
Ah, and we knew enough.
But-- and our professors at does at school did mention it.
We had superb professors.
Well, it started that year after I came back.
Now we go back maybe to 1938.
I grew like an asparagus, you know, very, very skinny and tall and had a little problem.
My-- my doctor was afraid I was catching tuberculosis.
Of course, I did not.
But I was advised to go on vacation in the mountains.
So I spend the summer the Jura Mountains, the way we did it at the time, pretty high.
It was a nice little hotel.
Had a nice time, except that in the evening, it was no television.
People talked.
And it was the time where there was a ship called the St.
Louis who was full of Jews and went-- I think it was the St.
Louis.
I was, you know-- it went to America.
And the Americans turned them out.
And they couldn't land.

And they had to be back to Germany, and of course-- and I remember, people were very-- an officer of the French army with his wife, and group of rich people said that Roosevelt had done well and should have sunk the ship all together so we get rid the all those scoundrels.
And you know, I couldn't believe it.
I didn't say anything.
Because as a child, you were not supposed to talk back to grown-ups.
But I was sick.
I was with my mother who was knitting with some other people.
But I happened to be there.
And I talked to my father.
And my father said it was the most horrible thing that those people said and the most horrible thing the United States had done.
And then we started to-- to worry about that.
Plus those orchestras with those sad eyes and those people who didn't play perfectly-- we knew when they made mistakes.
And they felt bad about it.
And, you know, the women were in their evening gowns, as supposed to be.
And I was envying them to travel from city to city and be all beautiful.
But I wonder why they look so sad.
And then my father explained.
Then it came the '38 to come back to school.
Then came the Crystal Night.
And the Crystal Night, my friend Rita Thalmann wrote the first book about it.
I think it's a classic.
And she mentioned it was November 9th that the German chose for a date for the fall of the Berlin Wall, the 9th of November, when it was not the 9th at all.
It was the 7th or it was the 11th, but it was not the 9th.
And they took the 9th to erase the other date.
I mean, nobody would know that.
But she does.
She notices those things.
And that was the Crystal Night.
And it was just an abomination.
We remember that my father was really sick over it.
Through the radio, the French radio--
--Or the Swiss radio.
We would listen always to the Swiss news, because it was closer to us.
Then in 1939, the war broke.
And it happened that in the fall, I went to see my friends at [NON-ENGLISH], same, who were living not too far.
I liked to spend-- I maybe had something to do in the German class or something.
Anyway, I went to see them.
And then the police came, the French police, to ask for the papers, what they were doing there, questions, you know, pictures.
And me too-- against the wall, what I was doing there.
Didn't I know that they were German, that I was doing something against my country?
The whole business-- you know, I was 16.
I was not feeling too well about that.
And it happens that my friend Rita has retired now.
And last summer, she decided-- not last summer, last year, she decided to write a autobiography, but not the way I would write it.
She's a historian, so she goes back to the archives.
So she went back to Dijon last summer.
It's recent.
She went through the archives.
And she found that I had a police record from that day.
And she found also that her father-- now I'll speak a bit of the Thalmanns.
They were pretty well-off Jews from Nuremberg for generations.
And their business was, from what we spoke about, mostly manufacture of underwear, heavy underwear, winter underwear, what you would call long-johns, things like that, that was sell at LL Bean's.
And the-- the father was not very gifted for business.
But that's beside the point.
So they had to move from Nuremberg.
They took whatever they could take, not very much.
But they took their-- those big things of underwear, you know, all this-- the store.
They brought it to [NON-ENGLISH].
And after a while, they were pushed out of [NON-ENGLISH].
They had to come to Dijon.
And that's where they lived not far from us.
And that's where Rita went to school with my mother.
And they were living about three, four houses from us.
Yeah.
And then she went to high school with me.
But she was in a lower grade.
And thinking of that, it just breaks my heart.
The father would take those clothes, you know, those underwear things in big suitcases in his car.
And he would-- he didn't speak French at all.
And he would stop at houses.
And Rita's mother, [NON-ENGLISH], who was, you know, those kind of Jewish women.
It's a special type-- very long, very thin with a thin face, the kind of face you see on [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, that kind of physique-- very delicate woman, a good pianist.
She had to lug those suitcases and do door to door.
She was the daughter of the Grand Rabbi of Basil, not raised for that at all.
And she had to go from door to door with those suitcases.

Because the women would not have opened the door to a man, mostly a man who didn't even speak French to begin with and looked very Jewish, very dark with a hook nose, you know, the typical caricature of the Jew.
That's the way he looked.
And so [NON-ENGLISH], who had such distinction and such poise, doors opened for her.
My mother bought more underwear that year.
We needed-- my father was glad to have it later on when we had no heat.
But anyway, she bought out of compassion, you could say.
None, we never-- we never did.
We never--
The Thalmanns were extremely orthodox.
I had to turn on their lights, you know, on Friday.
Or they had what you call the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, that kind of thing for the feast of the harvest--
--In the backyard.
Yeah.
And so I was invited for that.
But socially, they didn't meet with Gentiles at all.
No, not with them, because they were very orthodox.
Yeah, they bought underwear, because my mother was sorry for them.
It was things we didn't [? knew, ?] that kind of stuff.
And anyway, she was-- she had that haunted look.
She was very unhappy.
And he, the father, did not very much.
He didn't make much, but he was a good violinist.
So he played the violin in-- in movies, you know, in the movies.
Before the film played, you had somebody playing music.
He was doing that.
Poor man, really, but he had to make a little money.
And Rita, when she went back to Dijon last year, she looked at the police report.
And her father was described as a suspicious character with shady habits.
Well, the poor man was a poet, a violinist who only thought of his art and his music and did that underwear business because it was his parents' business, and he had to make a living.
I mean, he was-- he was nothing suspicious.
But you see, that was the climate.
So what he did, he enlisted into the French army when the war broke.
He did.
He didn't have to, but he did.
Because he felt he was going to secure his position and with the children.
Then we had the war.
And all that happened.
And then, I mean, not the war, we had what you call the exodus.
The German broke in.
Everybody fled including us, because we were bombed.
And on the roads, on the street, we saw Vietnamese, the little Vietnamese.
You know, they are small people.
We had taken them from our colonies and put them in the ammunition factories and all that.
And we abandoned them.
And there were thumbing their ways.
They looked like children to me.
I said to my dad, stop, stop.
But the car was overloaded with everything we had.
And you know, I left my heart with them.
So when the Vietnamese war came, you imagine on what side I was.
I mean, that's something I still--

We were heading for the south, for my uncle who was living well in the [NON-ENGLISH], where the old protestants used to live near the [NON-ENGLISH], and all that where my uncle's family lives even to this day.
So when we came near Lyon, the Italians machine gunned us.
And the car went tumbling.
And my parents were fine, my sister too.
But I was was sitting in the back with a huge jerrycan of gasoline.
Because we couldn't get gas.
All the gas station were burning.
All the houses were burning.
Everything was burning.
So we had that jerrycan, which didn't burst, which didn't burn anything, but broke my head.
So I had a fractured skull.
And I had to end up in the hospital.
And I woke up several days later.
My sister telling me the Germans are here.
And they are sewing patches on the seat of their pants on sewing machine on the public square.
And I said, what kind of crazy story is that?
And I was all bundled with things.
And they put me-- the sisters were horrible.
They cut my nails.
I couldn't even-- I had long braids.
They wanted to cut my hair.
But they didn't.
I fought, you know.
And they put me on a wheelchair with all my bandages, because I spoke German.
And I was wheeled all around with the German commandants to explain what the hospital was and blah blah.
Then I went back to Dijon eventually.
My father, my mother, and my sister, we went back.
We recovered our apartment.
And then I had to go take my exam with my crutches and my head all-- I looked like an Easter egg.
But I passed the exam very well.
And maybe they had pity on me or something.
But--
Oh, they were in German all the way to the end.
The flags, the red flags, the-- you know, the swastika was flying, even so low it just swept our faces.
And the Germans were all over.
But I have to say what you hear many French people say.
They were perfect.
Compared to the Americans who came later, you couldn't even step up of the house without having an American officer offer you cigarettes to go to bed with you.
The Germans, never.
The Germans were perfection.
They were carrying the suitcases of the old people.
They were told to be good.
And they won over the population somewhat.
Because they were very, very nice.
You talked earlier about the climate in Dijon before the--
Yeah.
The people--
The people, they were all for [NON-ENGLISH].
They were all for what the Catholic church said.
Of course, you had dissidents within the Catholic church.
But they were all for [NON-ENGLISH].
We were paying for the sins of the life we had before the war.
We had a good time.
We gave vacation to the workers.
We were dissolute.
And it was a punishment from God.
The Jews, we didn't know them then.
I mean, the Jews are all left.
They had gone with the French.
Some came back.
Had nothing to do with them, nothing--
Nothing.
No, no.
But they just were-- they avoided them, not so much as Jews as Germans.
Because we don't know what was what.
You know, we were told, stay away.
And that's true that the fifth column had infiltrated the Jewish community.
That's a fact.
They put spies.
They put spies who said they were Jews and went into the French, you know, played the card of refugees and they were not, you know, all that.
I don't know the details.
I don't know.
That I wouldn't know.
But anyway, it was a total disorganization.
To France.
--life was different.
It was under total German rule.
It was absolutely under German law, German rule.
We had the first traffic lights.
We never had a traffic lights, and particularly, no more cars.
The German had taken all the car.
We just had our bikes.
And the stores-- the Germans bought anything, you know, even gloves for their wives.
But they would buy all the sizes, all the shoes, everything that we had, all the butter.
They had a lot of money, because they had taken the presses to make bills at the Banque de France.
And they printed their own currency.
So they bought everything.
And at the time, I was approached by a neighbor-- not a neighbor, a woman I knew from the school.
And she said, you know, I have neighbors who are Jews.
And their name are [NON-ENGLISH].
But I don't know how to write it-- H-E-Y-M-A-N-N-- I'm not sure.
And I never met them except he.
They had to leave-- they were lawyers.
They were rich.
They had a beautiful apartment.
And they had to leave their apartment quickly.
They took all they could.
But then they went to the country, somewhere in people they knew in the back-country.
But they forgot jars of preserves in which they had hidden jewelry.
No.
I don't know.
But they were French.
They were French.
Maybe they came from the North, and they came down, you know.
And those jars were in the kitchen.
And the Germans were occupying that.
And they had no maids.
They had orderlies.
So they was delegated to get [LAUGHS] this jam.
So I went, you know, me knowing absolutely nothing, I went.
And the officers opened the door.
And I said that I was the daughter of the cleaning woman-- you bet-- and that, we had made preserves.
I had made my first preserve.
And we had forgot them.
And I wanted them.
Because they were the first one, I wanted to give to my grandmother.
I could lie like that.
And they marveled at my German.
And I said, yes, I learned German in school.
And I went there.
Oh, you went there.
And they take pictures of their children.
And we speak of Germany.
And they tell the orderly, you have those pots there in the kitchen?
Put a bag, and give it to her.
So I brought back of my-- my jars like that.
I gave it to the man.
And I never saw them again.
Do you know why they picked you to do it?
Because my-- the woman from the school knew I spoke French well.
And I had a braid around my head.
And I looked Germanic enough, I suppose.
And I was a nice kid.
And they took it for that.
They never suspected a minute that-- beside, it was food in a house of Jews.
They wouldn't want to eat that kind of stuff anyway.
So they took the furniture.
They used everything they could.
The silverware, of course, those people took with them.
But there were most of the pianos that remained.
But the jars of marmalade that they have put on a high shelf, they forgot.
Because they had to go too quickly.
I don't think it was all their jewelry But there was part of their jewelry that they had with them for security.
So I was able to get it back.
And I was very, very pleased.
[LAUGHS] That was-- maybe could have been dangerous, but I don't think so.
It was one of the things.
And then in 1941, the yellow star started to appear.
All the stores with they-- where Jewish had to have a yellow star in the window.
And we were advised not to shop there.
And to my great surprise, practically every store in Dijon was Jewish.
I didn't know that.
Nobody thought of it.
You know, when you saw stockings, underwear, perfume, sweaters, shoes, jewelry, beauty products, Helen, Helena Rubinstein, the store there-- all that was yellow star.
Don't go there.
My-- no.
My friends who were not Jewish didn't go there.
And beside, those poor people didn't have very much.
You know, the little they had was already taken by the Germans.
Oh, yes.
I never thought of it.
I remember a girl called Guggenheim.
We thought it was strange, but Guggenheim pronounced in the French way.
But we never thought of [?
Suzanne ?] Guggenheim being Jewish.
I mean, it never crossed our mind.
We never thought in those terms.
But all of a sudden, that appeared.
And then people had to wear the yellow stars.
And my-- my friend-- I wouldn't say school friends-- my schoolmates started to leave the school, the high school.
And we have a Bloch, Guggenheim, Frank, Oppenheimer, Jacob, Marx.
I mean, all these kids left.
Why did they leave?
It is because the principal asked them one-by-one to come to her office and spoke to them one-by-one telling them it was dangerous.
That if they wanted to stay in Dijon, that was not a good idea.
But if they did, she would manage to have teachers go into the house, continue the teaching.
But if the parents were wise, they should leave.
Rita left.
My friend Rita left the school.
And I have to say for our school teachers, they were extremely helpful.
They knew other teachers in other city, created some sort of a net.
And that's how Rita really could manage to go to Switzerland.
It was not through me.
It was through the teachers.
And our principal, who had been a lecturer at Bryn Mawr, an instructor in Bryn Mawr, and was now the-- you know, a high school in France is not a high school here.
It is very academic.
And it was you had an exam before you entered.
And it was like the gymnasium in Germany.
She was a very educated woman.
And she died in Ravensbruck.
She was Catholic.
And she was involved in mostly rescuing pilots, British or American pilots who, you know, had to fall with a parachute and had to be taken care of.
She was given away, and she died in Ravensbruck.
She was-- somebody informed on her.
She was an informer.
And she took-- she took care of a lot of Jews too.
I mean, when people took care of Jews, it was not because they were Jews.
It's because they were persecuted people and because the Nazi wanted them.
Yeah, yeah.
It was all a very loose thing.
Nobody gave their name to anybody.
Because in case you be caught, you would talk.
That they left?
We were told that the-- yeah, but we felt that they were going in a safe place, that they were going to the South, and they were going to the country.
Like our protestant ancestor, they were going somewhere where they wouldn't be caught.
As a matter of fact, quite a few came back.
No.
Except one, except one-- and that is a very sad story.
That's the story of Beatrice.
Beatrice came from Germany, from [NON-ENGLISH], And then she came to Alsace.
As then she came to Dijon.
I don't know for sure what her parents were doing, but they were rich.
And she was beautiful.
She had little button nose, curly hair, green eyes, teeth like pearls.
She was the most prettiest girl I had seen.
I mean, I am not a lesbian or anything, but I was sensitive to her beauty.
And she was fun.
And we were good friends.
And she invited me to her house.
And her house was the first house I saw with wall-to-wall carpeting.
We didn't have that at home-- and beautiful things.
And one day, she went to the library of her father where she had very nice bound book.
And she told me-- she gave me the book of-- a book by [NON-ENGLISH].
It was in German.
And she said, I give you that to remember me by.
Because I like that book.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] .
It's a novel.
And she said, you know, in France, you know, had this system.
I think it's very intelligent.
In school, we wore smocks.
They were ecru colored, you know, beige.
And we had our name embroidered.
But everybody was the same.
So whether you were rich or poor, when you were in school, you were all dressed the same.
And you left it.
We didn't have any locker, because there was no theft, nothing like that.
We left it on a hook with our class section.
And Beatrice told me, you know, tomorrow, I'm going to be here.
And you'll tell that I'm sick.
And in a week, you just take my smock.
And you can use it for yourself.
You just take off the name.
Because I have an aunt in Venezuela, and we are going.
So we kissed.
We cried, and OK.
And the next day, where's Beatrice?
Nobody knows.
No, Beatrice is ill.
OK, so all right, forgotten.
Maybe five days later, I go by the house of Beatrice.
And I see-- we have windows that open like that and shutters in metal.
And I saw beautiful lace curtains flying in the wind in the rain.
And I was-- I knew the mother of Beatrice.
I didn't know her much, but she was absolutely, you know, wonderful housewife.
Everything was in order.
And when the German took over a nice apartment, they didn't mess it up.
It was for their officers.
I find it was strange.
So I went to the concierge.
And I said, what happened to Beatrice?
Well, she said, my poor mademoiselle, you don't know?
They were all arrested.
Somebody told on them.
And the Nazi came.
And they were leaving.
And they had all their packs ready.
And they were taken.
OK.
We're going to have to change the tape--
Yes.
